item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The images were heavily modified from photos that are in the public domain.

The moon photo was adapted from the first photo ever taken of the far side of the moon (via the Soviet spacecraft Luna 3).

The red haze was adapted from a photo of clouds which I inverted and tuned. I can no longer find the original (it would be unrecognizable), but I verified that it was in the public domain before adapting it.

I have re-added the images to the article.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
http://www.peakpx.com/468183/red-and-yellow-clouds

CC0/public domain

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article could not be sourced, and thus are assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone else see a face in the lower right picture of the red clouds? Is that what we're supposed to see? I can't really determine anything else from the addenda, it just seems like it goes into left field and ends anticlimactically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn’t help but think about pattern screamers because first of all, the things mentioned are apparently outside of the universe, and second of all, Pond said that “they’re all crying”, which could be interpreted as either sobbing, or shouting/screaming. Also, that last Luna X image was swirly and red/black, it was reminiscent of the SCP-S image. And if the things were actually pattern screamers, it was blackboxed because, well, y’know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure those are pics of the Moon's far side, just distorted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the fundamental problem here is one I see fairly often on the site. Not every anomaly has to have a full reveal at the end, sometimes the mystery is way better (although I think sometimes a connection that can be understood and comprehended can be an asset to an article when you have a mythology reference or something, but there are differing opinions on that). But you lose people if they have NO idea what it could be. You want a level of detail that makes people go "Okay, so based off this, this, and this, could this be THIS?" but what we have is only "So…what is this?"

Perhaps you have some clues, but it's not clear enough what the clues are. Is the location relevant? The composition? The phenomena experienced by the individual in the log? The picture? I'm left not knowing enough to make an educated guess. And that kind of means I let go of any investment I had in this.

It's rather disappointing, too, because a mystery with enough detail to theorize about is SO much fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Picture creds, btw? They look like old school nuclear scintigraphy scans of … infant cranium? Badly wounded/diseased abdomen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And the foam is actually Brimstone, which isn't actually Sulphur as long expected… unless that's the "other substances" that are never named…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point of note on that, though - when descriptions are given such as the temperature / warmth being "twice" or "three times" what they were at the immediate exit of the wormhole, are we talking… celcius? kelvin? other? Like, have they increased to ~40 and then ~80'C, or several hundred?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta second that, still upvoting for the idea of the portal, that said I think this would loose nothing if you removed the 3rd exploration log and beyond, it's true mistery can add illusory depth but with this it's just unsatisfying.

Actually perhaps you should do that and replace it with details about the mist, drama like that is played out on this site a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main complaint with this is that portions of the additional addenda section ruin the direction of the narrative being told here, especially the last one. A secondary complaint is that the addenda tells instead of shows. I think introducing some other form of horror rather than the weird turn that -11 took would greatly improve this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way you described typical wormholes is only true of Einstein-Rosen bridges, i.e. black hole wormholes. However, the wormhole aspect of the anomaly would be better explained as an Ellis wormhole linking to a large mass in 2-D space that is able to exert a slight gravitational pull through the aperture but cannot itself be pulled through due to a combination of size and the dimensional difference. The gravitational lensing can also be explained through the basic warping of spacetime and three dimensions by the wormhole and what lies beyond, respectively.

Also yeah the logs could use significant revision but I'm not sure what specific advice to give in that regard.

I think this has interesting potential, but it needs work before I'm ready to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very interesting concept, and it was an engaging read I feel is worthy of an upvote in my book… but unfortunately the end was a bit too vague for my liking. You don't have to spoil everything if you think it would ruin the impact, but in my opinion giving readers a little bit more to work with and form at least some vague ideas as to what might be happening would help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last bit from O5-11 is… offputting, somehow. It seems redundant given the Foundation's goals, or else seems too much like personal butt-covering that could interfere with proper containment. Sorry, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, but please, if you know what's behind the blackboxes in that log, post it somewhere. The lack of finality is hurting me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did like this scp but my curiosity is eating me alive, I HAVE to know what was beyond the edge, what did it say in that log, what made the wormhole in the first place and why(does it seek to feed). You’ve said that you think that revealing what is on the other side would lessen the impact but considering how many people have complained BECAUSE it doesn’t say what’s out there that’s very unlikely(unless it really really sucks which I doubt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was mildly interested where you were taking this, though expecting either Things Man Was Not Meant To Know or some sort of Hell analogue, both of which have long since been played out. You instead subverted that expectation by providing no payoff whatsoever.

My emotional reaction is basically, 'meh,' and thus the article earns a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaand it's all red!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would you suggest I fix? I personally feel it's very nicely formatted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a valid opinion. Thank you for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the middle of outer space, he's correct in saying that 13.6°C really warm. However, I did write it correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comments.

Click me!
Unclick me!

how can it be unaffected by the moon's gravity yet itself have a gravitational field

That's exactly the point. It doesn't make sense, suggesting that the SCP is not a naturally-occurring phenomenon, nor does it abide by natural law. A possible second suggestion is that whatever created it (if it was created) stands outside of natural law as well.

how can you take a picture of it if it does not interact electromagnetically? how can you take a thermal image of it if it only emits trace amount of particle radiation (and therefore no heat)?

If you can think of a better way to word those parts, I'd be happy to change it. However, the idea was that the object is benign, whereas a typical "real-life" wormhole would be extremely radioactive and dangerous. SCP-3321, however, has minimal to no effect on compasses, and is safe to approach.

since its entrance is only 3 dimensional, which of the three dimensions does it have? x,y,z, and t are our options; so if it lacks t, then nothing would be able to enter it for starters…

how could they possibly know this

is this the 2 dimensional part? how can 4 dimensional objects exist and function in a 2 dimensional space? it lacks a direction and time…

Ultimately, science fiction. However, here are a couple things to consider. First, I specified that the entrance is at least 3-dimensional (can be entered from any direction), so it's basically a spherical hole. The exit, however, is more like a plate, which was described a bit in the exploration logs.

I footnoted the fact some Foundation scientists are exploring hyperconical explanations for this. If you consider that an ordinary cone is a 3-dimensional object with a 2-D base and a 0-D tip, then what if you up the numbers? If SCP-3321 is a 5-dimensional object, then it would have a 4-D entrance and a 2-D exit, and would exist simultaneously in multiple points across space.

In theory.

that is a pretty damn long tether for that camera

But not too long. ;)

clouds are tangible and also very heavy. the average weight of a cumulous cloud is ~1 million pounds.

Technically and technically. The point, however, is that it's thicker than a typical cloud. If you've ever walked outside on a foggy day, even though the air is humid, you don't really feel any resistance as you walk through it. However, the cosmic foam is not like that: it's thick, and you can literally feel it push against you as you navigate through it.

i find this absolutely ridiculous. there is no way any agency would allow a solo mission into something like a wormhole that is not at all understood. by this time, 5 years have passed but nothing is mentioned about the testing done on the foam samples that have been brought back.

First off, others have mentioned that they find it unlikely that the Foundation would conduct a solo expedition into an SCP. That's a valid concern, but they've done it before (admittedly more often with D-class). However, in order to change that, I would have to completely redo the third exploration log, which contains the meat of the story. That's not really something I'm comfortable doing at this point.

As for your second point regarding the five years passing, that's a good point as well. I'm not sure if it's too big of a deal, but it's a valid thing to bring up. I could probably add an additional addendum with a timestamp not long after Exploration Log #2. Do you think that's a good idea, or is it not really necessary?

stahp

pls

hmm full transcript, and all the important bits are blacked out. really makes you think.

I wrote "full transcript" because that's the entire transmission, obviously excluding the redaction. In other words, it was short, and that's the whole context. I could probably rewrite "full transcript" into "transcript" or "partial transcript" if that would be clearer. What do you think?

on what grounds? really gets the brain strained.

I have an answer in my head as to what's behind that redaction, and also why it concerns the Foundation. Some people think it's an annoying omission, but I think unmasking it would hurt the article more than leaving it as is.

All that said, again, thanks for the comments, and I'm glad you enjoyed the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, the idea is interesting. On the other hand, there's no climax and no "aha, that's so scary"/"aha, that's what's happening"/any sort of "aha" moment at the end. It just… peters out?
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 20°C is room temperature. Are you sure you read it correctly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mention the ambient temperature on the other side of the portal being 13.6°C, which is a lot. Are you sure those aren't in Kelvin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generally speaking, I assume when something is redacted/expunged, the in story reason is it's not for the eyes of whatever clearance the reader has or lower.

That said, that whole 'redacted = scary' cockblockery stopped being tolerable half way through Series 1. I feel like the story would work much better if the punchline was presented in so many words and careful redaction instead of straight out 'well, you figure it out'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

SCP-3321 shares a unique co-orbital configuration with the Moon about Earth, but is unaffected by the Moon's gravity.

It has a much weaker gravitational field than leading theories of relativity suggest is possible

how can it be unaffected by the moon's gravity yet itself have a gravitational field

SCP-3321 is very stable, has no electromagnetic field, and emits only trace amounts of particle radiation.

how can you take a picture of it if it does not interact electromagnetically? how can you take a thermal image of it if it only emits trace amount of particle radiation (and therefore no heat)?

It is possible to enter SCP-3321 from any direction in 3-dimensional space, and can be done so without causing damage to spacecraft.

since its entrance is only 3 dimensional, which of the three dimensions does it have? x,y,z, and t are our options; so if it lacks t, then nothing would be able to enter it for starters…

its exit exists in 2-dimensional space.

how could they possibly know this?

Once entered, SCP-3321 leads to SCP-3321-1, an area that is believed to be an uncharted location in deep space.

is this the 2 dimensional part? how can 4 dimensional objects exist and function in a 2 dimensional space? it lacks a direction and time…

A tethered camera was then extended into the surface of SCP-3321.

that is a pretty damn long tether for that camera

"It looks like a cloud, but… heavier, if that makes sense. Almost tangible."

clouds are tangible and also very heavy. the average weight of a cumulous cloud is ~1 million pounds.

Nearly five years after the previous voyage, a solo expedition was conducted into SCP-3321-1.

i find this absolutely ridiculous. there is no way any agency would allow a solo mission into something like a wormhole that is not at all understood. by this time, 5 years have passed but nothing is mentioned about the testing done on the foam samples that have been brought back.

Well… what if it actually is? What if SCP-3321-1 is the edge of the universe? What if all those theories about the universe being boundless are wrong, and I'm traveling towards the barrier between everything and nothing?

stahp

It looks kind of like… it looks like… oh my God.

[THE REMAINDER OF THIS AUDIO TRANSMISSION HAS BEEN REDACTED]

other than to hide what she sees from us, the reader, why would the foundation redact this part of the message in a *file made specifically to detail what it was*?

The full transcript is as follows:

Someone else is out here—no, everyone is. It's so loud, and they're all crying. I shouldn't have come. ███ ███ ██ █████ ███ █████ ████ ██ ████ ███ ████ ████████ ███ ████ █████████ █████████ ██ ███ ██████ ███ ██████ ███ ████ ███████████ ████. He wanted to show us, but why did you have to send me?

hmm full transcript, and all the important bits are blacked out. really makes you think.

"We have decided it is in the best interest of the Foundation that, frankly, nobody knows about that." —O5-11

on what grounds? really gets the brain strained.


For the sake of our mobile readers, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

all that aside, interesting read. i like scifi and i like seeing what interesting ideas people come up with for things having to do with space travel. i'm gonna give you an upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming the "Outside" is hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if I'm interpreting this correctly, the Edge of the Universe is…hell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Mortos said is exactly what I told you when I first reviewed your draft, author. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it enough to upvote, but please, for the love of God, fix that formatting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might have been interesting, but almost all of your logs and addenda just repeat information that's in the description without adding anything new, and the main hook of the article is redacted and never properly revealed.
